name: Peer-to-Peer (P2P)

forces: No central authority ↔ harder coordination / consistency
Scalability & resilience ↔ discovery, trust, and security
x.structure: Every node acts as both client and server; peers discover one another and share resources directly, often via a DHT or gossip, with no central coordinator.
antiPatterns: Assuming peers are trustworthy / always online
x.scale: Network / system; file sharing, blockchains, overlay networks, CDNs.

def: A decentralised topology where equal nodes act as both clients and servers, sharing resources directly without a central coordinator.
problem: Central servers are bottlenecks, single points of failure, and cost centres for large-scale resource sharing.
mechanism: Peers join an overlay and locate resources via structured (DHT) or unstructured (gossip/flooding) routing. Each node contributes storage/bandwidth/compute, so capacity scales with participants and there is no single failure point.
benefits: Scales with the number of peers
No single point of failure / central cost
failures: Sybil / eclipse attacks on the overlay
whenNot: Strong global consistency / central control needed
myths: "P2P means no infrastructure" — bootstrap nodes, NAT relays, and trust mechanisms are still needed
